title: A Line Along the Pacific
subtitle: Four days following Oregon’s edge, from the open headlands near Cannon Beach to the forested curves around Cape Perpetua.
opening: The road stayed close to the water. Each turn exchanged one scale for another: headlands giving way to tide pools, dark spruce above broad sand, and evening light settling into the coves. This is the coast in a handful of quiet frames—ordered southward, and kept close to what the camera could actually see.
closing: By dusk, the route had become a thin line folded back into the map.

destinations.name: Cannon Beach
destinations.introduction: A wide opening to the coast.

facts.text: Haystack Rock rises 235 feet above the beach and is part of the Oregon Islands National Wildlife Refuge.
The intertidal area at its base is protected as a marine garden during low tide.
Cape Kiwanda is the smallest of the Three Capes, formed from softer sandstone rather than basalt.
The cape’s shifting dunes and exposed cliffs make erosion part of the visible landscape.

destinations.name: Newport
destinations.introduction: Low tide reveals the smaller coast.

facts.text: Oregon’s rocky intertidal areas are layered habitats shaped by how long each zone remains exposed between tides.
Sea anemones close when exposed, retaining water until the returning tide covers them again.

destinations.name: Cape Perpetua
destinations.introduction: The forest meets the ocean at elevation.

facts.text: Cape Perpetua’s headland rises more than 800 feet above the Pacific, the highest accessible viewpoint by car on the Oregon Coast.
The surrounding scenic area combines old-growth forest, rocky shoreline, and protected marine habitat.

chapters.title: Where the road meets weather
chapters.subtitle: Northern coast
chapters.narrative: Marine haze held the horizon in layers. The first overlook made the route legible: beach below, dark rock offshore, and the coast folding south beyond the visible headlands.

chapters.title: At the scale of a tide pool
chapters.subtitle: Central coast
chapters.narrative: At low tide, the view turned downward. Mussels, anemones, and water-polished basalt formed a dense foreground while the sea stacks held their place farther out.

chapters.title: The last headland
chapters.subtitle: Cape Perpetua
chapters.narrative: The final section climbed through wind-shaped grass before the light withdrew. From the cabin, the same coast became a framed distance—blue water, warm timber, and one last line of sunset.

photos.alt: A broad Oregon beach and offshore sea stacks under late golden light
photos.caption: Evening light reaches a long beach beneath the northern headlands.

photos.alt: An empty trail crossing golden coastal grass above the Pacific
photos.caption: A narrow trail follows the exposed shoulder of the headland.

photos.alt: Green sea anemones and mussels in a basalt tide pool
photos.caption: The intertidal shelf at low water, with sea stacks beyond.

photos.alt: A warm cabin window framing a blue-hour view of the ocean
photos.caption: The coast at blue hour, seen from a warm cedar window.

photos.alt: Sea stacks receding into coastal haze
photos.caption: The headlands repeat southward in layers of mist.

photos.alt: Coastal grass and a winding path at Cape Perpetua
photos.caption: Late light crosses the grass above Cape Perpetua.

photos.alt: Tide pools among dark coastal rocks
photos.caption: A small basin holds the receding tide.

photos.alt: Ocean sunset through a cabin window
photos.caption: One final view before the map is folded away.
